heading: Getting Started
intro: How to access Claude and find your way around the interface.
steps: Go to claude.ai and sign in with your company email. SSO should log you in automatically.
You'll land on the chat interface. Type a message at the bottom and press Enter to start a conversation.
Your conversation history appears in the left sidebar. Each conversation is separate and independent.
To start fresh, click New Chat. Claude does not carry context between separate conversations.

cards.title: Chat area
cards.body: The main window where your conversation happens. You type at the bottom, Claude responds above. Attach files using the paperclip icon.

cards.title: Sidebar
cards.body: Your conversation history and Projects. Search past conversations and organise work into Projects for ongoing topics.

cards.title: Model selector
cards.body: Choose which Claude model to use at the top of each new conversation. The right choice depends on the task — more on this below.

cards.title: File attachments
cards.body: Click the paperclip to attach PDFs, Word docs, Excel files, images, or CSVs. Claude reads the content and works with it.

tip.label: If you've used ChatGPT
tip.body: The interface works similarly. Main differences are Projects (persistent context across conversations), different model names (Sonnet/Opus instead of GPT-4o), and how Claude handles longer documents. You'll pick it up quickly.

prompts.title: Finance starting point
prompts.body:
I have a monthly management accounts pack that I present to the board. The pack includes P&L, balance sheet, cash flow, and a narrative commentary. I spend about 3 hours writing the commentary each month.

Here is last month's commentary [paste it]. And here is this month's data [attach the file].

Draft this month's commentary in the same style and format. Flag anything that looks unusual compared to last month.

prompts.title: Management starting point
prompts.body:
I need to prepare a briefing note for our CEO on the Q3 operational performance of our three business units. I'll give you the KPI dashboards for each unit.

For each unit, summarise the top 3 things going well, the top 3 concerns, and any metrics that have moved more than 10% vs prior quarter. Keep the whole thing under 2 pages.

[Attach the dashboards]

prompts.title: Quick productivity win
prompts.body:
I have a meeting in 30 minutes with the Head of Procurement about consolidating our software vendors. Here are my rough notes [paste them].

Turn these into:
1. A clear agenda with time allocations
2. The 3 key decisions we need to make
3. A list of questions I should ask to understand their constraints

models_intro: The model selector appears at the top of each new conversation. Picking the right one affects the speed, depth, and quality of the response. For most tasks, the default is fine. But knowing when to switch makes a difference.

models_cards.title: Sonnet — default
models_cards.body: Fast, capable, and the right choice for 90% of tasks: drafting, summarising, analysing documents, coding help, and general Q&A. Start here unless you have a reason not to.

models_cards.title: Opus — for complex work
models_cards.body: Slower and more thorough. Use it when depth matters more than speed: strategic analysis, evaluating competing arguments, complex financial modelling, or any task where nuance and reasoning quality are critical.

models_cards.title: Haiku — for speed
models_cards.body: The fastest and lightest model. Good for simple, repetitive, or time-sensitive tasks where you need a quick answer and the task doesn't require deep reasoning.

thinking_body: When using Opus, you may see an option to enable extended thinking. When switched on, Claude works through the problem step by step before giving you its answer — similar to showing its working. This makes a meaningful difference on tasks that involve weighing up multiple options, identifying logical gaps, or producing outputs that need to be well-reasoned and defensible.

thinking_tip.label: When to use it
thinking_tip.body: Turn thinking on for complex analysis, strategic trade-offs, evaluating business cases, or any task where you want to understand Claude's reasoning — not just the conclusion. Turn it off for speed-sensitive tasks like drafting a quick email or summarising a document. The tradeoff is time: thinking mode takes noticeably longer.

preferences_why: By default, Claude behaves like a polished but generic assistant. It over-explains, hedges, uses filler phrases, and writes in a style optimised for everyone — which often means it doesn't quite work for anyone. Preferences let you permanently reshape how Claude communicates with you. Set them once and every future conversation benefits automatically. For a busy professional, this is one of the highest-value things you can do in the first five minutes.
preferences_steps: Click your profile icon at the bottom left of the sidebar on claude.ai.
Find the Profile section and look for how Claude responds or user preferences.
Write your instructions in plain English. No special formatting needed.
Save. These apply to all your future conversations automatically.
preferences_example_body:
WRITING STYLE
Write for a senior business audience. Use UK spelling and a concise, professional tone. Keep responses practical, structured and easy to reuse in emails, decks, agendas and discussion papers.

LANGUAGE AND GRAMMAR
Keep sentences short and clear. No em-dashes, no Oxford comma in lists, no ampersands. No icons or emoji in any context.

TONE
Avoid hype, marketing language and unnecessary technical detail. Translate complex topics into business language. Be clear about implications, risks and trade-offs. No filler affirmations like "great question" or "certainly". Just answer.

REWRITING
Preserve my intent. Make wording sharper, not more corporate.

STRATEGIC QUESTIONS
Give a recommendation, not just options.

LENGTH AND FORMAT
Default to short sections and simple lists. Do not over-explain. If I want more detail I will ask.

CITATIONS
Use numbered hyperlinked citations [1], [2] and include a References section with source name, URL and date. Use exact URLs from search results. Do not invent URLs.

preferences_tip.label: Where to start
preferences_tip.body: Begin with 3-4 clear instructions and add more as you notice patterns in Claude's default behaviour that don't suit you. Think about: tone (formal or conversational), length (brief or detailed), structure (bullets or prose), and specific habits to avoid. The more specific you are, the more consistent the results.
